Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered. I'd try to set aside time in the evenings after my other responsibilities, but it rarely worked out consistently. I'm an auditory learner, so I spent a lot of time listening to lecture recordings and discussion podcasts, sometimes while doing other things. I'd have bursts where I'd study intensely for a few days, then life would get in the way and I'd fall behind again. With nine online courses, organizing everything felt overwhelming most of the time.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. I wanted to be consistent, but between my extracurricular commitments and just general life stuff, regular times were hard to maintain. Deadlines would hit and suddenly I'd be cramming, then I'd crash afterward. Some weeks I'd manage 18 hours spread out, other weeks it was all concentrated into two or three desperate nights.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or getting a notification from a peer in the discussion forums. The peer support system actually helped motivate me to log in more than I would have otherwise. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel connected to the course, even if I wasn't sure what I needed to do. The discussion posts from other students made me feel less alone in struggling with the material.
Q4: For a while, checking the discussion forums became almost habitual for me. I'd log in to see what my peers were posting and respond to their questions or share my own confusion. That social element made the LMS feel less like a chore and more like a community space. But when my anxiety got bad, even that routine fell apart because interacting with others felt exhausting.
Q5: The video lectures were my lifeline because I could listen to them repeatedly. I'd often play them while doing other tasks, letting the information sink in through repetition. The discussion forums were also valuable because hearing other students explain concepts in different ways helped things click for me. When someone would break down a difficult topic in their own words, it was sometimes more helpful than the official materials.
Q6: I often skipped the reading materials because without internet at home, downloading and reading PDFs was a hassle. The written assignments piled up because I'd get anxious about starting them and then avoid them entirely. I know my assignment completion was really low, and it wasn't because I didn't care—I just froze when facing those tasks. Some resources felt redundant after watching the videos, so I'd convince myself I didn't need them.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could immediately see how it connected to understanding the bigger picture. If I could listen to it or if it involved interacting with peers, I was more likely to engage. When something felt like busywork or when the purpose wasn't clear, my motivation dropped. I needed to feel like I was actually learning, not just checking boxes.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford with everything else on my plate. Required ones carried the weight of grades, so they got priority even when I was overwhelmed. But honestly, sometimes the optional activities were more interesting because there was no pressure attached. The anxiety around required work sometimes made me avoid it more than the optional stuff.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture audio files when I had access and listen to them repeatedly at home or during commutes. I borrowed textbooks from friends and took handwritten notes that I'd review later. A lot of my actual learning happened through conversations with study partners that never showed up in any system.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or listening to downloaded lectures for hours, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd have long study sessions with peers from my extracurricular groups where we'd work through problems together. Sometimes I'd be mentally processing course concepts while doing other things, which is real learning but completely invisible to any tracking system.
Q11: Yes, this was my main approach actually. I'd access a video once, download the audio, and then listen to it multiple times offline. Without reliable internet, I had to work this way. One download might turn into five or six listens over the following week. The LMS would only show that single access even though I engaged with that material repeatedly.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where my anxiety spiked and everything felt impossible. When assignments started piling up and I fell behind, the sadness would set in and logging in felt like facing my failures. My attendance dropped significantly during those times. The peer support helped pull me back sometimes, but there were weeks where I just couldn't face it.
Q13: All of those factors hit me at different points. Managing nine courses was genuinely overwhelming, and fatigue was constant. When I was confused about material, my anxiety would spike and I'd avoid the LMS rather than seek help. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars sometimes meant the coursework got pushed aside. The workload felt designed for someone with more resources and stability than I had.
Q14: Many times I'd be logged in, watching videos, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing anything because I was too anxious or sad. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open while worrying about other things in my life. Those sessions looked like engagement but were really just me going through motions.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety around them was paralyzing—I'd think about starting and then feel this wave of dread. It wasn't laziness; it was fear of not doing well enough combined with feeling overwhelmed. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow would come with the same avoidance.
Q16: Deadlines were the main thing that forced me into action, but they also caused the most stress. I'd have periods of panic-driven activity right before due dates. The problem was that by then, I often didn't have enough time to do quality work. My whole engagement pattern was shaped by deadline pressure rather than steady progress.
Q17: When I got feedback, it could go either way. Positive feedback motivated me to keep trying, but critical feedback sometimes sent me into a spiral. I'd avoid checking grades for days because I was scared of what I'd see. The peer feedback in discussions was easier to handle than instructor feedback because it felt less judgmental.
Q18: The course structure with so many components felt overwhelming for someone juggling multiple courses. Having clear deadlines helped in some ways but also created stress clusters. The discussion forum structure was actually helpful because it gave me a social anchor. But the overall pace assumed a level of consistency I couldn't maintain.
Q19: The video lectures being available for download made engagement possible for me given my internet situation. The discussion forums being active made the course feel human. But the assignment structure with multiple components was hard—I'd complete parts but not submit because I couldn't finish everything. The lack of flexibility in deadlines made it harder when life got complicated.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well actually. I was highly engaged in watching videos and participating in discussions, but my assignment submissions were terrible. For me, it was anxiety about the assignments themselves, not lack of interest or effort. Some students might be genuinely learning and engaging but struggling with the output part for various reasons—mental health, life circumstances, or just different strengths.
Q21: I can see how that happens—some people are just efficient or learn well from minimal interaction with the system. They might read something once and understand it, or they might be using external resources heavily. For me, I needed high LMS activity to learn, but it didn't translate to performance because of my other struggles. Different students have different paths to understanding.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access and time spent, which indicates something about effort. They can show participation in discussions and consistency of logins. But they miss the quality of that engagement, the learning happening offline, and the emotional state behind the numbers. They can't tell if someone is genuinely absorbing material or just going through motions while struggling internally.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material and making effort to learn, even when it's hard. It's about showing up mentally, not just physically logging in. For me, engagement also means connecting with peers and feeling part of a learning community. It's possible to be deeply engaged emotionally and intellectually while still struggling to produce visible results.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually cared about learning despite my poor grades. It wouldn't show the hours I spent listening to lectures offline or studying with friends. It wouldn't capture my anxiety attacks before assignments or the sadness I felt watching my grades suffer despite my efforts. The peer support that kept me going, the resilience it took to keep logging in after failures—none of that shows up in the data.
